diagrams.title: String Methods Classification Architecture
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing categorization of additional string methods in Java
elements: Root node: Additional String Methods
Branch 1: Whitespace Handling (trim(), strip(), stripLeading(), stripTrailing())
Branch 2: Substring Operations (substring(), subSequence())
Branch 3: Parsing/Splitting (split(), StringTokenizer - related)
Branch 4: Replacement (replace(), replaceAll(), replaceFirst())
Branch 5: Validation (matches())
Branch 6: Formatting (format(), valueOf())
Leaf nodes showing method signatures with parameters
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to categorize string methods by their functionality, making it easier to recall and apply the right method for specific tasks

diagrams.title: substring() Method Index Visualization
diagrams.description: Visual representation showing how substring() extracts portions of a string using start and end indices
elements: String: 'WELCOME' displayed horizontally
Index numbers shown below each character: W[0], E[1], L[2], C[3], O[4], M[5], E[6]
Arrow pointing from index 0 to index 3 labeled 'startIndex = 0'
Arrow pointing from index 5 to index 7 labeled 'endIndex = 7' (exclusive)
Result boxes showing: substring(0,3) = 'WEL', substring(3) = 'COME'
Highlighted regions showing extracted portions in different colors
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how string indices work in Java (0-based, endIndex is exclusive) and practice extracting substrings correctly

diagrams.title: split() Method Delimiter Processing Flow
diagrams.description: Flow diagram showing how the split() method processes a string using delimiters
elements: Input String: 'apple,banana,cherry' with comma delimiter
Processing box: 'Tokenize by delimiter'
Arrow showing delimiter ',' identification
Split points indicated by vertical lines between tokens
Output: String array ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'] with indices [0], [1], [2]
Additional flow for limit parameter: split(regex, limit) showing difference between limit=2 and no limit
Visual comparison: split(",") vs split(",", 2)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how split() breaks strings into arrays, the role of delimiters, and how the limit parameter affects output

diagrams.title: trim() vs strip() Whitespace Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison diagram showing differences between trim() and strip() methods in handling Unicode whitespace
elements: String with leading and trailing whitespace: ' Hello World '
Unicode character breakdown showing various whitespace: regular space, tab, newline
Left side: trim() flow showing only ASCII whitespace removal (<= U+0020)
Right side: strip() flow showing Unicode whitespace removal (Character.isWhitespace())
Result comparison boxes for each method
Key difference callout: strip() is Unicode-aware, recommended for modern Java
Additional visual: stripLeading() and stripTrailing() methods
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the differences between legacy and modern string whitespace removal methods and when to use each

charts.title: replace() Methods Comparison Chart
charts.content: Table with columns: Method, Regex Support, Replacement Type, Example Input, Output, Notes. Rows: replace(CharSequence, CharSequence), replaceAll(String regex, String replacement), replaceFirst(String regex, String replacement). Shows literal vs regex matching, first occurrence vs all occurrences differences.

charts.title: String Method Selection Flowchart
charts.content: Decision tree starting with 'Need String Manipulation?' -> 'What type?' -> Branches: 'Remove whitespace' -> trim()/strip() decision; 'Extract portion' -> substring(); 'Split by delimiter' -> split(); 'Find and replace' -> replace/replaceAll; 'Validate pattern' -> matches(); 'Format output' -> format(). Each branch shows method signature and return type.

charts.title: matches() vs contains() vs equals() Methods
charts.content: Table comparing: matches() - checks entire string against regex pattern, returns boolean; contains() - checks substring presence, returns boolean; equals() - exact string comparison, returns boolean. Includes use cases: email validation, password checking, search operations.

charts.title: String Methods Return Type Classification
charts.content: Chart categorizing methods by return type: Returns String: trim(), strip(), substring(), replace(), replaceAll(), format(), toLowerCase(), toUpperCase(), concat(); Returns String Array: split(); Returns boolean: matches(), contains(), startsWith(), endsWith(), isEmpty(); Returns int: indexOf(), lastIndexOf(). Color-coded by category.

branches: Whitespace Handling: trim(), strip(), stripLeading(), stripTrailing()
Replacement: replace(), replaceAll(), replaceFirst() - literal vs regex
Utility: valueOf() - data conversion to String
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all additional string methods, showing their relationships and helping students recall the complete method set for string manipulation tasks in Java
